name: Qebehsenuef
description: Qebehsenuef is one of the four sons of Horus in Egyptian mythology, and a funerary deity. He is associated with the canopic jar that protected the intestines of the deceased during the mummification process. Qebehsenuef is typically depicted with the head of a falcon. His role was to safeguard the intestines and ensure the deceased had them in the afterlife. He was under the protection of the goddess Serket. As one of the four sons of Horus, he played a crucial part in the resurrection of Osiris, and he helps the deceased achieve rebirth. He represents the west and is associated with the element of air. He is considered a protector of the dead and a guide in the afterlife, ensuring the body remains whole and ready for resurrection. Qebehsenuef's falcon head symbolized foresight and protection, guiding the deceased safely through the underworld. He also represents the power of transformation and renewal, vital for the journey to the afterlife. He is an important figure in funerary rituals and beliefs about the afterlife in ancient Egypt.
subtitle: Falcon-headed protector of the intestines

keyMyths.title: Protection of the Intestines
keyMyths.description: Qebehsenuef protected the intestines, placed in his canopic jar, ensuring the deceased had them for the afterlife. He worked under the goddess Serket's protection.

keyMyths.title: Son of Horus
keyMyths.description: As one of the four sons of Horus, Qebehsenuef played a vital role in funerary rituals and the resurrection of Osiris. He aided the deceased's journey to the afterlife.

keyMyths.title: Association with the West
keyMyths.description: Qebehsenuef is associated with the west, symbolizing the direction of death and the setting sun. He guards the deceased on their journey to the underworld.

sources.author: E.A. Wallis Budge
